chunks.title: Catalog Description
chunks.content:
Software Engineering. (2-2). Credit 3. Application of engineering approach to
computer software design and development; life cycle models, software
requirements and specification; conceptual model design; detailed design;
validation and verification; design quality assurance; software design/development
environments and project management. Prerequisite: Grade of C or better in CSCE
315 or CSCE 331 or approval of instructor.

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content:
Prerequisite/Corequisite(s): Grade of C or better in CSCE 315 or CSCE 331 or
approval of instructor.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:
• Apply the engineering approach to the design and development of computer
software systems.
• Explain major software life cycle models and their appropriate use in project
contexts.
• Document software requirements and specifications in a clear, testable, and
structured form that captures user needs.
• Design conceptual and detailed software models that align with requirements
and specifications.
• Implement and test software modules to verify functional correctness against
documented requirements and validate them against user needs and project
goals.
• Evaluate software products and processes for quality assurance, including
planning for risks and quality, and for conformance to relevant external and
internal organizational standards.
• Use industry-standard software development and project management tools
to plan, monitor, and deliver software projects.
• Function effectively as part of a software engineering team, demonstrating
professional responsibility and clear communication with stakeholders.

chunks.title: Special Course Designation
chunks.content: None

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content:
This material Is: Optional
Software Engineering: A Practitioner's Approach.
Authors: Roger Pressman
Publisher: McGraw-Hill
Publication Date: 2019
Edition: 9th

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
The course simulates a software development organization with performance expectations that
operationalize the Course Learning Outcomes listed above. Each performance expectation below
is aligned with one or more Course Learning Outcomes (LOs).
• Understand factors that contribute to high quality software, including processes,
methods, tools, and risk mitigation, monitoring, and management across the
software engineering lifecycle from conception through deployment and
support.
Success is measured by the team being able to deliver the most important
features to stakeholders by the end of the semester according to the
agreed to timeline, and at least a satisfactory stakeholder or user
feedback**
In support of this outcome, below are sub-outcomes:
• Evaluate stakeholders' requirements as a basis for the design,
implementation, and validation of a computing-based solution, with
success measured by reaching at least an average satisfactory
stakeholder or user feedback**.
• Build a computing-based solution, evaluate (e.g., test, review), using an
appropriate software architecture and deployment environment, with
success measured by zero high severity (i.e., no workarounds) errors
discovered by stakeholders.
• Apply the most effective communication techniques to function effectively as
part of a team, with people you have not worked with before, who may have
diverse backgrounds/personalities, with success measured by at least
satisfactory peer feedback**.
• Answer questions (impromptu or not) about the software engineering
lifecycle, software engineering processes, methods, and tools; and how they
contribute to developing high quality software, as indicated by the ability to
communicate a clear, succinct answer with specific supporting examples.
** The feedback scores are from pre-defined surveys administered by the instructor.
This class is a 4-credit hour class and meets officially for a little over 4 hours per week (250
minutes). For every one credit hour in which you enroll, you will spend 2-3 hours outside of class
studying. Therefore, a reasonable course load (including project work) will consume on average
6-9 hours outside of class. Total commitment for this class is 10 to 13 hours per week averaged
across the semester.
The final grade you will receive in the class will be based on points accumulated during the
semester. Thus, both continued progress on the process and the quality of your results will
decide your grade.
The grading scale is A ≥ 90% > B > 80% > C ≥ 70% > D ≥ 60% > F.
We make every effort to provide you with the opportunity to earn an A (including opportunities for
extra credit), so at the end of the course, an 89.9 is a B. We do not curve in this class. Rather, we
teach proficiency which means that we have clear expectations in terms of what you should
complete and what knowledge you should have obtained by the end of the course to succeed
and to earn an A. If you are contributing appropriately and improving based on our feedback, you
should expect an A.
Breakdown of the total percentage points in the class
| Category | Percentage |
|---|---|
| • Professionalism & Continuous Improvement - Engagement, communication, reliability, and use of feedback to improve your work and teamwork over the semester. | 25% |
| • Project Sprint 0 -15% Sprint 1 - 3% Sprint 2 - 7% Sprint 3-10% Project Turnover – 15% | 50% |
| ○ Governance - Understanding and applying project management, process, and quality practices to plan, monitor, and control the team's software engineering work. | 25% |
| Category | Percentage |
|---|---|
| Extra Credit**: See separate section below. | Depending on the complexity or time involved, 1-5 % point(s) is added to the final grade. For example, producing an educational video for the benefit of other students can be worth 1% points. |
Group Project Grade
You will work in a project group to develop a software application for intended users or
stakeholders, applying an engineering approach to design, development, validation,
documentation, and an end-of-semester class demonstration of the solution. The focus is
not only on the final product but also on the process, project management, and teamwork.
The course simulates a software development organization, with you playing the role of a
member of a project team. You will develop an application using appropriate software
architecture and deployment environments for your intended users or stakeholders. Each
project group must use the Ruby on Rails framework and PostgreSQL (and/or alternative
technology as approved by the instructor).
The instructor team will act as managers and mentors.
The class will be divided into project groups. To ensure inclusiveness and avoid students
feeling excluded, you will be assigned to a project group based on your lab section and
the results of a skill survey (to be given when class starts) to help distribute students with
prior experience in particular technologies evenly across project groups.
Project rubrics, used to assess your class performance, and a project notebook to guide
you in the software engineering process will be distributed once the project groups are
formed. Additional grading criteria may be added to the initial rubric; these will count as
extra credit, and you and/or your team are under no obligation to fulfill the additional
criteria. You can still earn a grade of A by fulfilling the basic requirements.
Because your teammates' grades are tightly coupled with your own commitment to the
class, your grade is contingent on your equitable contribution to producing project
deliverables as described in the project rubric. Expectations include:
• Software made available to intended users or stakeholders. A key
prerequisite to getting an A in the class is the delivery of a tested
working system that meets stakeholders' expectations (as indicated by
stakeholder or user feedback of at least "meets expectations") by the
end of the semester.
• Final code that is clean and understandable submitted in the assigned
GitHub Classroom repository at the end of the semester, along with
any required supporting documentation (for example, readme, code
comments, and similar materials).
• Submission of assigned documents (for example, requirements,
designs, and project-management-related documents) to support
understanding and proper execution of your system.
• Submission of a high-quality slide deck and recording of the final
presentation, evaluated on the clarity of communication and
demonstration of your system. These will be viewed by other students
in the class and will be used as the basis for their end-of-semester
reflection.
• Submission of reflections that are relevant and thorough (using the
given template).
• Submission of other artifacts as required by the instructor (for
example, project management related reports).
• Presentation of incremental progress during a project audit in each
sprint conducted by the instructor team. These audits are mandatory
and will be recorded. Students who are absent (see section “Graded
Absence" below) will receive a 30% deduction from the group sprint
grade unless the absence or lateness is considered excused.
• Submission of all required peer feedback and completion of all project
deliverables on the dates specified in Canvas; these deadlines are
intentional to ensure there is sufficient time to complete grading.
• Final course grades will not be released until all project deliverables are
verified and accepted by the instructor team.
Your project grade can increase, based on (but not limited to) the following:
• Excellent project deliverables that exceed basic expectations.
• Excellent peer feedback and contribution to the group project.
• Effective, polite, and collaborative communication with the
instructor/teaching team.
Your project grade can decrease, based on (but not limited to) the following:
• Poor stakeholder feedback or deployment of an application with high-
severity defects (that is, with no acceptable workaround).
• The instructor team's perception of your effort. This can be due to poor
peer feedback from your peers (after the validity of the feedback is
confirmed), other measures of contribution, or other factors at the
instructor team's discretion.
• Regularly missing your group meetings without notice.
• Failure to complete required peer feedback on time.
• Unprofessional or inappropriate (for example, rude) communication
with instructors or classmates.
Individual Contribution
• This represents your individual proficiency during one individual project
audit conducted towards the end of the semester, which will assess your
knowledge on a specific topic(s) in software engineering.
• Each team member is expected to commit to the team's GitHub repository
on a regular basis as specified in the project rubric.
• You are expected to thoroughly test / review your code and develop
automated tests.
• Extra credit is given for those who assume the Product Owner and Scrum
Master role, if the team grade is >=80%, and stakeholder/user feedback
and peer feedback score are at least meets expectations.
• Each member of your project team will score and give feedback on the
performance of the other team members through periodic peer feedback
forms throughout the semester. Your peer feedback responses will be
calculated along with the instructors' peer feedback scores and will be part
of your individual project grade. NOTE: The instructor team has the
right to withhold credit for late submission of peer feedback unless
you have prior approval for late submission. You will receive two
reminders. Failure to submit after the second reminder will result in
a 5% deduction from your final grade, and cannot be reversed once
the peer feedback grades have been posted in Canvas. This policy is
in place to ensure timely distribution of valuable peer feedback.
Quizzes
Frequent quizzes will be given and will cover material from the homework, lectures, and
any pre & post lecture work. These assessments let us know where your knowledge
might be lacking so that we may be able to make class adjustments and inform you about
the knowledge that you need to have.
Once you have taken the quiz, you are not allowed to talk about its composition with
anyone. Any unauthorized sharing is considered academic misconduct and will be
reported to the honors council and result in the application of an appropriate sanction. In
addition, quizzes must be done in class, otherwise, submission when not in
attendance can constitute academic misconduct.
Assignments
• Some assignments are expected to be completed individually, otherwise
over collaboration may be considered academic misconduct and will be
reported to the honors council and will result in the application of an
appropriate sanction.
• Assignments are usually due at midnight of the due date through Canvas,
unless otherwise noted. Email submissions will not be accepted.
Extra Credit
When needed to reinforce proficiency in concepts, extra credit assignments may be
assigned, which will *all* count as extra credit. There is no obligation for you to do these
assignments, rather these should be viewed as opportunities to boost your grade. Details
on extra credit opportunities will be announced.
Depending on the complexity or time involved, extra credit is worth additional percentage
point(s) added to the final grade.
To be eligible for extra credit points, you need to meet the eligibility criteria described
below.
• Attendance must be 4 out of the 5 each from lab and lecture (i.e., in
the Attendance category, your % points should be >= 4%, out of 5%).
• At least a satisfactory peer and stakeholder feedback rating
• Instructor discretion based on individual and team performance.

chunks.title: Graded Attendance
chunks.content:
Since groups will be working on the project during lecture and/or lab, attendance is
important, hence attendance is graded according to the following guidelines:
• Attendance will be measured through one or more of the following ways: electronically
(e.g., Canvas Roll Call), hardcopy attendance sheets, quizzes, and submission of in-
class exercises. At the end of the semester, 5 random days in the semester will be
chosen as basis for attendance, for both lab and lecture.
• Absence and Tardiness: Failure to attend a class or late arrival (by
definition > 5 minutes late) or leaving class early (> 5 minutes before
class ends) without prior approval, may be counted as an absence and will
impact your grade. Lateness or absence can be excused per student rule
7. Please inform the instructor at your earliest opportunity of your
absence / tardiness in the event communication channels are down or you
cannot inform in advance.
• Due to the unfortunate consequences and undue burden your frequent
absences would have on the rest of the team, more than 5 unexcused
absences from either lecture or lab are an automatic forfeit of the
team grade.
• Excuses Not Valid: Oversleeping, cramming, etc., are not valid reasons.
The instructor reserves the right to determine what constitutes a "valid
reason" on a case-by-case basis.
• There will be no makeup for attendance unless the absence meets student
rule 7.

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not
considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).

chunks.title: Course Specific Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
No "unapproved' late assignments will be accepted without prior approval and will receive a
grade of 0. No late work will be accepted after the last day of class, irrespective of the deadline
for an assignment.
With pre-approval from the instructor, late submissions are accepted only up to the time that the
solutions will be discussed in class or as agreed to with the instructor depending on the specific
circumstance. Late work approvals must clearly link to an excused absence request per Student
Rule 7.
The request for deadline extensions should be submitted at least three business days before the
due date, by sending a request via a link to be given to you by the instructor, especially for those
absences that meet student rule 7. This will allow us time to review your request and ask for
additional information when needed. Technology-related / technological problems nor failure to
confirm correct submission are not an excuse for incomplete or late assignments.
*The only exception is in case of an emergency, where you cannot request in advance. In this
case, you can submit details of your absence when you are able to.
Request for late submissions not related to absences can be considered. The instructor will base
their approval on how well it meets the criteria described in Student Rule 7.

chunks.title: Course Schedule
chunks.content:
Schedule subject to change. Check Canvas for the most up-to-date schedule and additional
details. Labs will be conducted weekly unless otherwise announced.
| Week # | Main Themes | Major Deliverables / Milestones |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Overview of the course; Introduction to software engineering & Agile; Intro to Rails | Skill Survey; Syllabus & Class Appreciative Agreement; Appearance Release |
| 2 | Project overview; Requirements & design; Testing & Quality; TDD introduced | Project groups announced; Team Working Agreement; Customer scope discussion |
| 3 | Risk analysis & stakeholder management; CI/CD; Quality techniques | Sprint 0 Scope, Backlog, UAT form, Risk Plan, and Data Design due |
| 4 | Correctness & Compliance; Ensuring integrity through authentication | Learning Outcomes Pre-Survey; Week 3 Lab; Book Collections due; Sprint 1 begins |
| 5 | Creating maintainable code; Usability & UX design; Efficiency; Preparing for Sprint 1 | Sprint 1 Project Notebook partial submission |
| 6 | Sprint 1 audits and reviews | Sprint 1 Deliverables & Deployment due; Sprint 1 Project Audit, Review, Retrospective; Sprint 2 planning and scope due |
| 7 | Project management - estimation, monitoring & control; Deployment & support planning | Peer Feedback 1; Sprint 2 Project Notebook partial submission |
| 8 | Sprint 2 audits and reviews; Sprint 3 begins | Sprint 2 Deliverables & Deployment due; Sprint 2 Project Audit, Review, Retrospective; Sprint 3 planning and scope due |
| 9 | Spring Break - No class | (Spring Break) |
| 10 | Preparing for User Acceptance Testing (UAT); Configuration management; Maintenance overview | Sprint 3 Scope, Backlog, Risk Plan, UAT form due; Mock UAT setup; Mid-semester evaluation |
| 11 | Project close overview; Project presentation timeline; Project turnover report | Sprint 3 Project Notebook partial submission; Peer Feedback 2; Quality of peer review evaluation |
| 12 | Project turnover; Final sprint ends; Customer UAT begins | Sprint 3 Deliverables due; Sprint 3 Project Audit, Review, Retrospective; Maintenance & Project Turnover phase begins |
| 13 | Project presentations; Course evaluation | Project Presentations (3 groups); Quality post-surveys (Compliance, Integrity, Correctness); CSE Capstone Expo |
| 14 | Project presentations (continued); Future of software engineering; Course evaluation | Project Presentations (continued, 3 groups); Quality post-surveys (Usability, Maintainability); Project Turnover Notebook partial submission; Customer UAT ends |
| 15 | Final project turnover deliverables; Maintenance phase ends; Official project close; Last lab day | Project Turnover Deliverables due; Reflection on presentations; Official Project Close; Extra Credit Educational Video due |

chunks.title: Additional Course Information
chunks.content:
Course expectations, in the form of a course appreciative agreement, will be reviewed and
agreed to in the first 2 weeks of class.

chunks.title: Course Copyright
chunks.content:
The materials used within this course are copyrighted and include, but are not limited to, the
syllabi, quizzes, exams, homework, online handouts, course videos, audio, and visual recordings
of classes, etc. Because these materials are copyrighted, you do not have the right to copy or
distribute these materials, unless permission is expressly granted.

chunks.title: AI Statement
chunks.content:
Writing: Use of AI tools, including ChatGPT, is permitted in this course for students who wish to
use them. To adhere to our scholarly values, students must cite any AI-generated material that
informed their work (this includes in-text citations and/or use of quotations, and in your reference
list). Using an AI tool to generate content without proper attribution qualifies as academic
dishonesty.
See https://www.chicagomanualofstyle.org/qanda/data/faq/topics/Documentation/faq0422.html for
information how to properly cite AI tools. (adapted from University of Massachusetts
Amherst, https://cte.tamu.edu/getmedia/1d5e4ef6-97f1-4065-987f-3c9dfecbb7bd/TAMU-
CTE GenAI-SyllabusStatementConsiderations.pdf)
Computer Code. In principle you may submit AI-generated code, or code that is based on or
derived from AI-generated code, as long as this use is properly documented in the comments:
you need to include the prompt and the significant parts of the response. AI tools may help you
avoid syntax errors, but there is no guarantee that the generated code is correct. It is your
responsibility to identify errors in program logic through comprehensive, documented testing.
Moreover, generated code, even if syntactically correct, may have significant scope for
improvement, in particular regarding separation of concerns and avoiding repetitions. The
submission itself must meet our standards of attribution and validation.(from Boris Steipe (2023)
“Syllabus Resources”. The Sentient Syllabus Project http://sentientsyllabus.org..)

chunks.title: University Policies
chunks.content:
This section outlines the university-level policies that must be included in each
course syllabus. The TAMU Faculty Advisory Council established the wording of
these policies.

chunks.title: Academic Integrity Statement and Policy
chunks.content:
"An Aggie does not lie, cheat or steal, or tolerate those who do.”
“Texas A&M University students are responsible for authenticating all work
submitted to an instructor. If asked, students must be able to produce proof that
the item submitted is indeed the work of that student. Students must keep
appropriate records at all times. The inability to authenticate one's work, should
the instructor request it, may be sufficient grounds to initiate an academic
misconduct case” (Section 20.1.2.3, Student Rule 20).
You can learn more about the Aggie Honor System Office Rules and Procedures,
academic integrity, and your rights and responsibilities at aggiehonor.tamu.edu.

chunks.title: University Attendance Policy
chunks.content:
The university views class attendance and participation as an individual student
responsibility. Students are expected to attend class and to complete all
assignments.
Please refer to Student Rule 7 in its entirety for information about excused
absences, including definitions, and related documentation and timelines.

chunks.title: Makeup Work Policy
chunks.content:
Students will be excused from attending class on the day of a graded activity or
when attendance contributes to a student's grade, for the reasons stated in
Student Rule 7, or other reason deemed appropriate by the instructor.
Please refer to Student Rule 7 in its entirety for information about makeup work,
including definitions, and related documentation and timelines.
Absences related to Title IX of the Education Amendments of 1972 may necessitate
a period of more than 30 days for make-up work, and the timeframe for make-up
work should be agreed upon by the student and instructor” (Student Rule 7,
Section 7.4.1).
"The instructor is under no obligation to provide an opportunity for the student to
make up work missed because of an unexcused absence” (Student Rule 7, Section
7.4.2).
Students who request an excused absence are expected to uphold the Aggie Honor
Code and Student Conduct Code. (See Student Rule 24.)

chunks.title: Notice of Nondiscrimination
chunks.content:
Texas A&M University is committed to providing safe and non-discriminatory
learning, living, and work environments for all members of the University
community. The University provides equal opportunity to all employees, students,
applicants for employment or admission, and the public, regardless of race, color,
sex (including pregnancy and related conditions), religion, national origin, age,
disability, genetic information, or veteran status.
Texas A&M University will promptly, thoroughly, and fairly investigate and resolve
all complaints of discrimination, harassment (including sexual harassment),
complicity, and related retaliation based on a protected class in accordance with
System Regulation 08.01.01, University Rule 08.01.01.M1, Standard Administrative
Procedure (SAP) 08.01.01.M1.01, and applicable federal and state laws. In
accordance with Title IX and its implementing regulations, Texas A&M does not
discriminate on the basis of sex in any educational program or activity, including
admissions and employment.

chunks.title: Civil Rights, Free Speech, and Title IX Policies
chunks.content:
The following person has been designated to handle inquiries and complaints
regarding the non-discrimination policies: Jennifer M. Smith, TAMU Associate VP &
Title IX Coordinator at YMCA Ste 108, College Station, TX 77843, 979-458-8407, or
email civilrights@tamu.edu. For other reporting options, visit the U.S. Department
of Education Office for Civil Rights Complaint Assessment System to locate the
address and phone number of the office that serves your area, or call 1-800-421-
3481.
Texas A&M University is committed to fostering a learning environment that is safe
and productive for all. University policies and federal and state laws prohibit
discrimination and harassment based on an individual's race, color, sex, (including
pregnancy and related conditions), religion, national origin, age, disability, genetic
information, veteran status, or any other legally protected characteristic. This
includes forms of sex-based violence, such as sexual assault, sexual harassment,
sexual exploitation, dating/domestic violence, and stalking.
Students can report discrimination/harassment, access supportive resources, or
learn more about their options for resolving complaints on the University's Civil
Rights & Title IX webpage.
Students should be aware that all university employees (except medical or mental
health providers) are mandatory reporters, which means that if they observe,
experience or become aware of an incident that they reasonably believe to be
discrimination/harassment alleged to have been committed by or against a person
who was a student or employee at the time of the incident, the employee must
report the incident to the university.

chunks.title: Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA) Policy
chunks.content:
Texas A&M University is committed to providing equitable access to learning
opportunities for all students. If you experience barriers to your education due to a
disability or think you may have a disability, please contact the Disability Resources
office on your campus (resources listed below). Disabilities may include, but are not
limited to, attentional, learning, mental health, sensory, physical, or chronic health
conditions. All students are encouraged to discuss their disability-related needs
with Disability Resources and their instructors as soon as possible.
To request academic accommodations, contact the designated ADA office based
on your location:
• Texas A&M University, College of Nursing, College of Dentistry, Irma Lerma
Rangel College of Pharmacy College Station, College of Medicine, School of
Public Health, Institute of Biosciences and Technology, EnMed Program, Bush
School in Washington DC, Mays Business School – CityCentre, TAMU
Engineering Academies, Texas A&M University Higher Education Center at
McAllen and Texas A&M University at Galveston should contact Disability
Resources at (979) 845-1637 or disability@tamu.edu.
• Texas A&M University School of Law should contact the Office of Student
Affairs at (817) 212-4111 or law-disability@law.tamu.edu to request
accommodations.
• Irma Lerma Rangel College of Pharmacy in Kingsville should contact the
Disability Resource Center at Texas A&M University-Kingsville at (361) 593-
3024 or drc.center@tamuk.edu to request accommodations.
• Texas A&M University College of Veterinary Medicine & Biomedical Sciences in
Canyon should contact the Office of Student Accessibility at West Texas A&M
University – Canyon at (806) 651-2335 or osa@wtamu.edu.
If you are experiencing difficulties with your approved accommodations, contact
the office responsible for approving your accommodations or the Texas A&M ADA
Coordinator Julie Kuder at ADA.Coordinator@tamu.edu or (979) 458-8407.

chunks.title: Pregnancy Accommodations
chunks.content:
Texas A&M provides reasonable accommodations to students due to pregnancy
and/or related conditions, such as childbirth, recovery, and lactation. Students
should contact the University's Pregnancy Coordinator as soon as they become
aware of the need for accommodation. Depending on the circumstances,
accommodations could include extended time to complete assignments or exams,
changes in course sequence, or modifications to the physical classroom
environment.
Texas A&M will also allow a voluntary leave of absence, ensure the availability of
lactation space, and maintain grievance procedures to provide for the prompt and
equitable resolution of complaints of sex discrimination. For information regarding
pregnancy accommodations, email TIX.Pregnancy@tamu.edu.

chunks.title: Statement on Mental Health and Wellness
chunks.content:
Texas A&M University recognizes that mental health and wellness are critical
factors influencing a student's academic success and overall wellbeing. Students
are encouraged to engage in healthy self-care practices by utilizing the resources
and services available through University Health Services. The TELUS Health
Student Support app provides access to professional counseling in multiple
languages anytime, anywhere by phone or chat, and the 988 Suicide & Crisis
Lifeline offers 24-hour emergency support at 988 or 988lifeline.org.
Texas A&M College Station
Students needing a listening ear can contact University Health Services at
979.458.4584. Call 911 or visit your nearest emergency room if you are currently
experiencing a life-threatening situation or if your safety is at risk. 24-hour
emergency help is also available through the 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline (988) or
at 988lifeline.org.

chunks.title: Statement on the Family Educational Rights and Privacy Act (FERPA)
chunks.content:
FERPA is a federal law designed to protect the privacy of educational records by
limiting access to these records, to establish the right of students to inspect and
review their educational records, and to provide guidelines for the correction of
inaccurate and misleading data through informal and formal hearings.
Currently enrolled students wishing to withhold any or all directory information
items can do so within howdy.tamu.edu using the Directory Information
Withholding Form. The complete FERPA Notice to Students and the student
records policy is available on the Office of the Registrar webpage.
Items that can never be identified as public information are a student's social
security number, citizenship, gender, grades, GPR, or class schedule. All efforts will
be made in this class to protect your privacy and to ensure confidential treatment
of information associated with or generated by your participation in the class.
Directory items include name, UIN, local address, permanent address, email
address, local telephone number, permanent telephone number, dates of
attendance, program of study (college, major, campus), classification, previous
institutions attended, degrees, honors and awards received, participation in
officially recognized activities and sports, medical residence location, and medical
residence specialization.

chunks.title: Free Speech and Civil Discourse
chunks.content:
Texas A&M recognizes that the pursuit of truth through open and robust discourse
is critical to academic inquiry. However, as a community of scholars, the university
has an aspirational expectation that such discourse will be conducted in
accordance with Aggie Core Values. In this “marketplace of ideas,” we encourage
civil dialogue creating an environment that allows individuals to express their ideas
and to have their ideas challenged in respectful and responsible ways. Students can
learn more about Freedom of Expression and Free Speech on the University's
website about the First Amendment.
